summary: DHH criticizes Speednames, a Danish domain registrar that coasted on early market advantage and customer lock-in without improving their service. The company became more inconvenient (requiring faxed paper forms) and more expensive than competitors. DHH argues that businesses must compete on at least one dimension—service, price, or convenience—or face inevitable decline. The post reflects his early thinking on competitive dynamics and customer retention.
key_points: Early market advantage and customer lock-in are not substitutes for continued effort and improvement
Businesses must compete on at least one of three axes: better service, lower prices, or more convenience
Adding friction (like requiring faxed paper forms) while raising prices is a recipe for losing customers
Customer inertia only delays the inevitable—it doesn't prevent competitors from winning
money_quotes: With a ton of purchases already locked up in their management system, they didn't have to work very hard to keep my business. Unfortunately, they didn't work at all.
Either you provide better service, lower prices, or more convenience. Or you decent into irrelevance is only determined by the time lag that exists until your customers figure this out.
key_insight: Customer lock-in only buys time—businesses that fail to compete on service, price, or convenience will inevitably lose to those that do.
